scientificName: Lactuca sativa var. longifolia 'Parris Island Cos'
whatToFeed: Mild organic fish emulsion or balanced liquid seaweed fertilizer.
pruningTips: Harvest individual outer leaves early for salads, or let the central core mature into a full upright head.
flavorProfile: Crisp, sweet, and refreshing with a robust, crunchy central leaf rib.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow seeds or plant transplants in mid-April; repeat in mid-August for a fall crop.
whenToHarvest: Harvest the entire head by cutting it 1 inch above the soil line when it reaches 8-10 inches tall and feels tightly packed.
usesForLargeHarvests: Classic Caesar salads, grilled romaine hearts, green juicing blends.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual; tolerates light spring and autumn frosts well.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds 1/8 inch deep directly into fine, loose topsoil, spacing seeds 2 inches apart. Once seedlings develop a few leaves, thin them to a final spacing of 8 inches apart. Water lightly but frequently to keep the surface moist.
growthRequirements: Requires loose, nitrogen-rich, well-aerated soil with a pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Demands constant moisture to prevent leaves from turning bitter or bolting. Prefers full sun but thrives with afternoon shade when temperatures rise.

pruningTips: Use the 'cut-and-come-again' method by clipping leaves 1 inch above the base to allow rapid regeneration.
flavorProfile: Exceptionally mild, tender, and sweet with a soft, delicate texture.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow early April through May, and repeat in late August for an autumn harvest.
whenToHarvest: Harvest individual loose leaves at any size once they reach 4 inches in length.
usesForLargeHarvests: Colorful spring salad mixes, garnishes, fresh artisanal leaf bundles.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual; features excellent heat resistance that delays bolting compared to other varieties.
plantingInstructions: Direct sow seeds 1/8 inch deep in rows spaced 12 inches apart. Dust lightly with fine soil and press gently. Thin the emerged seedlings to a final spacing of 6-8 inches apart to allow the open rosettes room to fan out.
growthRequirements: Thrives in rich, high-compost, moisture-retentive loam with a neutral pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Requires reliable, uniform hydration to preserve leaf tenderness. Intense sun deepens the red pigmentation, but afternoon shade prevents wilting.

whatToFeed: Diluted organic fish emulsion or liquid kelp extract.
pruningTips: Harvest outer leaves continuously to keep the central growing bud active and producing fresh foliage.
flavorProfile: Crisp, sweet, and distinctly grassy with a refreshing, light crunch.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors as soon as the soil can be worked in early April; sow sequentially every 2 weeks.
whenToHarvest: Snip individual fronds once they are large enough to use, or harvest the entire loose bunch before a central stalk forms.
usesForLargeHarvests: Daily tossed green salads, lining sandwich platters, green smoothies.
hardinessNote: Fast-growing cool-season annual; easy to manage for consecutive cropping.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds shallowly at a depth of 1/8 inch in well-tilled soil. Keep the bed uniformly moist with a fine spray nozzle. Thin out the seedlings to stand 6 inches apart once they reach 2 inches in height.
growthRequirements: Prefers shallow, loose, well-drained sandy loam heavily amended with rotted compost, maintaining a pH of 6.0 to 6.8. Requires a steady supply of water to keep shallow roots cool. Thrives in bright morning sun and afternoon shade.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic vegetable fertilizer rich in nitrogen and trace minerals.
pruningTips: Clear away any yellowed or rotting bottom wrapper leaves to prevent soil-borne fungi from invading the core head.
flavorProfile: Mild, extremely watery, and exceptionally crisp with a classic refreshing crunch.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Start seeds indoors early March; transplant outdoors in mid-April to ensure harvest before summer heat spikes.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the central globe feels hard, solid, and tightly packed when pressed gently with fingers.
usesForLargeHarvests: Wedge salads, shredding for tacos and burgers, cold food storage displays.
hardinessNote: Grows as a cool-season annual; highly sensitive to heat spikes which trigger tip-burn and internal rotting.
plantingInstructions: Start seeds indoors 4-6 weeks ahead of time to give plants a head start against summer weather. Transplant the seedlings 10-12 inches apart in rows spaced 18 inches apart, taking care not to bury the central crown.
growthRequirements: Demands deeply cultivated, moisture-retentive, fertile soil with a stable pH of 6.2 to 6.8. Requires absolute consistency in watering; erratic moisture causes heads to crack open. Needs full sun, but cool ambient temperatures.

whatToFeed: Heavy feeder; apply nitrogen-rich fertilizer like fish emulsion early on, shifting to a balanced organic feed as heads form.
pruningTips: Do not remove healthy leaves, as they shield the inner head. Cut away flower stalks immediately if the plant attempts to bolt.
flavorProfile: Mildly sweet, peppery, and crisp when fresh, transforming into a rich, sweet, savory flavor when cooked.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Transplant outdoors in mid-April for a summer crop, or plant in early August for a fall harvest.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the round head feels rock-hard and solid when squeezed firmly.
usesForLargeHarvests: Homemade sauerkraut fermentation, bulk coleslaws, traditional cabbage rolls, winter root-cellar storage.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual; fall-grown crops become noticeably sweeter after experiencing a few autumn frosts.
plantingInstructions: Set 4-to-5-week-old transplants 18 inches apart in rows spaced 24 inches apart. Bury the stalk slightly deeper than its original container line to anchor the heavy plant structure. Water deeply immediately.
growthRequirements: Requires heavy, fertile, water-retentive clay-loam soil rich in organic humus, with a preferred pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Demands uniform, deep moisture parameters to prevent heads from splitting open unexpectedly. Full sun is essential.

whatToFeed: High-nitrogen organic liquid feed supplemented with potassium for firm head development.
pruningTips: Keep the area completely clear of weeds to optimize airflow around the heavy, low-slung crimson heads.
flavorProfile: Robust, earthy, and notably more peppery and sharp than traditional green cabbage.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Set out transplants in mid-to-late April. Highly recommended for autumn gardens by planting in late July.
whenToHarvest: Cut from the base when the deep purple-red globe feels exceptionally firm and reaches the size of a grapefruit.
usesForLargeHarvests: Pickling in spiced vinegar, braising with apples, adding vibrant color contrast to shredded slaws.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual; red pigmentation intensifies and sugars release upon exposure to frosty weather.
plantingInstructions: Space young nursery starts 12-15 inches apart in rows spaced 2 feet apart. Firm the surrounding soil down aggressively around the roots to anchor the plant against high winds. Mulch heavily to keep soil temperatures down.
growthRequirements: Thrives in deep, nutrient-dense, well-draining garden beds rich in rotted manure, maintaining a pH scale of 6.5 to 7.2. Requires a steady supply of moisture and complete sun exposure to foster tight, symmetric head formation.

whatToFeed: Compost tea mixed with high-nitrogen organic fertilizer every two weeks during active vegetative growth.
pruningTips: Gently clear away any yellowed or insect-damaged outer crinkled leaves at the baseline of the plant.
flavorProfile: Milder, sweeter, and less sulfurous than smooth cabbage, with a tender yet crisp, non-waxy texture.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Best grown as a fall crop; transplant outside in mid-July to allow heading during cool autumn months.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the crinkled head becomes relatively firm, though it will remain naturally looser than standard green types.
usesForLargeHarvests: Gourmet stuffed cabbage rolls, stir-frying, adding texture to winter vegetable soups.
hardinessNote: Remarkably winter-hardy cool crop; crinkled leaves are highly frost-resistant and insulate the head down to 15°F.
plantingInstructions: Set robust transplants 18-24 inches apart in rows 30 inches apart to accommodate its massive, spreading outer foliage canopy. Anchor roots firmly and irrigate heavily to support fast leaf scaling.
growthRequirements: Demands rich, moisture-retaining loam holding massive amounts of organic compost, with a preferred pH between 6.5 and 7.5. Needs constant, deep hydration and full sun exposure to fully knit the elaborate, ruffled leaf layers.

whatToFeed: Balanced organic fertilizer high in nitrogen to promote fast, lush leaf development.
pruningTips: Protect against pests continuously; do not prune leaves, as the upright barrel shapes rely on tightly folded inner growth.
flavorProfile: Delicate, sweet, and mild with a juicy, watery crunch concentrated in the wide, crisp white ribs.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant out in early August for a fall crop. Highly prone to bolting if planted in spring as days lengthen.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the oblong, barrel-shaped head feels solid and substantial when pressed from the sides.
usesForLargeHarvests: Making authentic homemade kimchi, Asian slaws, hot pot additions, stir-fries.
hardinessNote: Cool-season annual that thrives best in declining autumn day lengths.
plantingInstructions: Transplant seedlings 12 inches apart in rows spaced 18 inches apart during early August. Handle the delicate roots gently to avoid transplant shock, which can cause the plant to bolt prematurely without heading.
growthRequirements: Requires rich, loose, highly porous soil with excellent drainage and a pH range of 6.5 to 7.0. Shallow root systems demand frequent, shallow watering routines to prevent drying out. Thrives in full sun to partial shade.

whatToFeed: Organic fish emulsion or nitrogen-heavy liquid feed to encourage fast, thick succulent stems.
pruningTips: Harvest the entire cluster at once, or break off individual large outer stalks to use as a cut-and-come-again vegetable.
flavorProfile: Crisp and juicy white stalks with a sweet, mild flavor, paired with tender spinach-like green leaves.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Direct sow outdoors mid-April for spring, or sow mid-August for a fast, reliable fall crop.
whenToHarvest: Harvest when the base forms a tight, heavy, vase-like clustering rosette, before any central flower stalks emerge.
usesForLargeHarvests: Garlic stir-fries, halving and grilling, pickling, addition to ramen and noodle broths.
hardinessNote: Fast-growing cool crop annual; highly susceptible to bolting if exposed to summer heat or dramatic spring temperature shifts.
plantingInstructions: Sow seeds directly into the garden 1/4 inch deep, spaced 1 inch apart. Once sprouted, thin the young plants to a final spacing of 6-8 inches apart for baby bok choy, or 12 inches apart for full-sized varieties.
growthRequirements: Thrives in loose, light, highly fertile loam with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. Demands continuous, uniform soil moisture to keep the thick, fleshy stalks juicy and tender. Grows well in full sun but benefits from partial shade in warm weather.
